interest: Teacher unions seeking protection against uncompensated workload increases, and non-state (church/foundation) maintainers seeking to preserve their existing admission discretion over mission-relevant criteria.
value: Procedural fairness and institutional autonomy in tension with centrally-mandated equity implementation (voluntary/negotiated design vs. imposed reform).
fear: Teachers fear absorbing unfunded assessment-redesign and heightened compositional heterogeneity as added load, while maintainers fear a decree replacing their mission-based discretion with an externally imposed SES proxy.
assumption: That a pilot imposed without proportional resourcing or negotiated participation would degrade teaching capacity and provoke maintainer opt-out, and that voluntary, negotiated design is both legally and politically available.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual premise is mixed: it is settled that no current legal mechanism compels non-state maintainers to adopt uniform criteria, but the workload/resource impact of SES-driven compositional change is contested and would only be quantified by pilot data.
